source.notes: Optimus _ One Logistics  - 2026_05_14 10_59 EDT - Notes by Gemini.docx

call_summary: Max Riemer (Optimus) demoed a freight-brokerage-specific data platform to Victor Louis of One Logistics, a decisive TQL-veteran broker doing ~$40M/year who is actively unhappy with ZoomInfo and deciding on tooling imminently. Victor dominated the conversation (60% of words) and repeatedly praised the product, but pushed back hard on org-wide seat pricing, questioned whether the contact data actually beats ZoomInfo, and gated any larger spend on a CRM the platform doesn't yet have. The call closed on a concrete, time-boxed next step: Max sends a trial link for a week-long evaluation of contact-data quality, and Victor will circle back on a ~5-user, per-seat deal.

delivery_engagement.explanation: Max sounded genuinely engaged and responsive rather than scripted — he asked live follow-ups to Victor's exact points and reframed his pitch on the fly when Victor objected that reps wouldn't adopt the tool. Acoustics back this up: healthy pitch variation (8.21 st) and arousal/valence running above his own 44-call baseline (z ≈ +0.9 / +0.8), so the low-key tone reflects an easygoing style, not disengagement.

evidence.quote: does that mean they're just too operationally heavy?
One is a lot of the wins that we see from Optimus are not early wins at least. The easiest wins are account management.

value_prop_clarity.explanation: The reason-to-care never crystallized into a crisp, quantified sentence — Max led with data-sourcing mechanics and price rather than outcome, and offered no ROI figure to justify the spend. Critically, the differentiator he emphasized (contact data) was one Victor immediately dismissed as a commodity, so the value did not land.

evidence.quote: So sticker price there is 1,500 a month and that is unlimited users, unlimited access.
It's all all the s***'s linked in for the most part.

relevance.explanation: Max tailored the pitch to One Logistics' cradle-to-grave model and existing-account base rather than delivering a generic tour, and tied the use case to their capacity/lane situation with a concrete backup-freight example.

evidence.quote: The easiest wins are account management.
whoever you call Organic Valley and you say, "Hey, Lefthar, Wisconsin, like we're we're going into there a few times a week. I see that y'all are moving out of there.

discovery_progression.explanation: Max asked productive, position-advancing questions (competitor terms, the reps' actual pitch) and landed a concrete, time-boxed next step. It was slightly buyer-controlled — no specific follow-up call was locked — but a real action and window were set.

evidence.quote: What did uh what did Zoom offer you?
I'm going to shoot you a link right after this call for to log in and you can just play around for the rest of the week.

Max Riemer.coaching_action: Steer the trial toward your real differentiator — freight-specific shipper lane and volume data for expanding existing accounts ('you run these lanes, not those other five') — and get Victor to evaluate that, instead of letting him benchmark you on contact-data quality, which he already treats as a ZoomInfo-equivalent commodity where you can't win.

signals.signal: Buyer-owned next step: committed to personally run a trial to benchmark the data
signals.quote: I want to run through it myself to kind of cross reference to see how good the contacts are

signals.signal: Named a specific user to onboard immediately
signals.quote: I do have one user that I'd be interested in kicking this to like right away because he just started with me

signals.signal: Pricing objection — would stay with the incumbent at the quoted price for his org
signals.quote: I'd probably stay with Zoom Vin info at that for my organization.

signals.signal: Larger commitment gated on a missing feature (CRM)
signals.quote: there was a CRM in it I'd be much more interested or I'd be, you know, I could justify a spend of that size.
